title: Length of a degree of longitude

infobox.key: Lines of longitude appear [vertical](/wiki/Vertical_direction "Vertical direction") with varying curvature in this projection, but are actually halves of great ellipses, with identical radii at a given latitude.

Lines of latitude appear [horizontal](/wiki/Horizontal_plane "Horizontal plane") with varying curvature in this projection; but are actually circular with different radii. All locations with a given latitude are collectively referred to as a [circle of latitude](/wiki/Circle_of_latitude "Circle of latitude").
infobox.value: | The **[equator](/wiki/Equator "Equator")** divides the planet into a [Northern Hemisphere](/wiki/Northern_Hemisphere "Northern Hemisphere") and a [Southern Hemisphere](/wiki/Southern_Hemisphere "Southern Hemisphere"), and has a latitude of 0°.

divides the planet into a [Northern Hemisphere](/wiki/Northern_Hemisphere "Northern Hemisphere") and a [Southern Hemisphere](/wiki/Southern_Hemisphere "Southern Hemisphere"), and has a latitude of 0°. |

([/ˈlɒndʒɪtjuːd/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"), AU and UK also [/ˈlɒŋɡɪ-/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"))[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) is a [geographic coordinate](/wiki/Geographic_coordinate_system "Geographic coordinate system") that specifies the [east](/wiki/East "East")-[west](/wiki/West "West") position of a point on the surface of the [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth"), or another [celestial](/wiki/Celestial_navigation "Celestial navigation") body. It is an [angular measurement](/wiki/Angular_measurement "Angular measurement"), usually expressed in [degrees](/wiki/Degree_(angle) "Degree (angle)") and denoted by the [Greek letter](/wiki/Greek_alphabet "Greek alphabet") [lambda](/wiki/Lambda "Lambda") (λ). [Meridians](/wiki/Meridian_(geography) "Meridian (geography)") are imaginary semicircular lines running from [pole](/wiki/Geographical_pole "Geographical pole") to pole that connect points with the same longitude. The [prime meridian](/wiki/Prime_meridian "Prime meridian") defines 0° longitude; by convention the [International Reference Meridian](/wiki/International_Reference_Meridian "International Reference Meridian") for the Earth passes near the [Royal Observatory in Greenwich](/wiki/Royal_Observatory,_Greenwich "Royal Observatory, Greenwich"), south-east London on the island of [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain"). Positive longitudes are east of the prime meridian, and negative ones are west.

can differ slightly from the ordinary longitude because of *[vertical deflection](/wiki/Vertical_deflection "Vertical deflection")*, small variations in Earth's gravitational field (see [astronomical latitude](/wiki/Astronomical_latitude "Astronomical latitude")).

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Length of a degree of longitude](/w/index.php?title=Length_of_a_degree_of_longitude&redirect=no "Length of a degree of longitude"))

East-West geographic coordinate

For other uses, see [Longitude (disambiguation)](/wiki/Longitude_(disambiguation) "Longitude (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Longitude_blue.svg/250px-Longitude_blue.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Longitude_blue.svg)

Earth with blue longitude lines and corresponding degrees.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Division_of_the_Earth_into_Gauss-Krueger_zones_-_Globe.svg/250px-Division_of_the_Earth_into_Gauss-Krueger_zones_-_Globe.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Division_of_the_Earth_into_Gauss-Krueger_zones_-_Globe.svg)

A [graticule](/wiki/Geographic_coordinate_system "Geographic coordinate system") on the [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth") as a [sphere](/wiki/Sphere "Sphere") or an [ellipsoid](/wiki/Ellipsoid "Ellipsoid"). The lines from pole to pole are lines of constant longitude, or [meridians](/wiki/Meridian_(geography) "Meridian (geography)"). The circles parallel to the [Equator](/wiki/Equator "Equator") are circles of constant [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude"), or [parallels](/wiki/Circle_of_latitude "Circle of latitude"). The graticule shows the latitude and longitude of points on the surface. In this example, meridians are spaced at 6° intervals and parallels at 4° intervals.

**Longitude** ([/ˈlɒndʒɪtjuːd/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"), AU and UK also [/ˈlɒŋɡɪ-/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"))[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) is a [geographic coordinate](/wiki/Geographic_coordinate_system "Geographic coordinate system") that specifies the [east](/wiki/East "East")-[west](/wiki/West "West") position of a point on the surface of the [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth"), or another [celestial](/wiki/Celestial_navigation "Celestial navigation") body. It is an [angular measurement](/wiki/Angular_measurement "Angular measurement"), usually expressed in [degrees](/wiki/Degree_(angle) "Degree (angle)") and denoted by the [Greek letter](/wiki/Greek_alphabet "Greek alphabet") [lambda](/wiki/Lambda "Lambda") (λ). [Meridians](/wiki/Meridian_(geography) "Meridian (geography)") are imaginary semicircular lines running from [pole](/wiki/Geographical_pole "Geographical pole") to pole that connect points with the same longitude. The [prime meridian](/wiki/Prime_meridian "Prime meridian") defines 0° longitude; by convention the [International Reference Meridian](/wiki/International_Reference_Meridian "International Reference Meridian") for the Earth passes near the [Royal Observatory in Greenwich](/wiki/Royal_Observatory,_Greenwich "Royal Observatory, Greenwich"), south-east London on the island of [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain"). Positive longitudes are east of the prime meridian, and negative ones are west.

Because of the [Earth's rotation](/wiki/Earth%27s_rotation "Earth's rotation"), there is a close connection [between longitude and time measurement](/wiki/Time_zone "Time zone"). Scientifically precise [local time](/wiki/Local_time "Local time") varies with longitude: a difference of 15° longitude corresponds to a one-hour difference in local time, due to the differing position in relation to the Sun. Comparing local time to an absolute measure of time allows longitude to be determined. Depending on the era, the absolute time might be obtained from a celestial event visible from both locations, such as a lunar eclipse, or from a time signal transmitted by telegraph or radio. The principle is straightforward, but in practice finding a reliable method of determining longitude took centuries and required the effort of some of the greatest scientific minds.

A location's north-south position along a meridian is given by its *[latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude")*, which is approximately the angle between the [equatorial plane](/wiki/Equatorial_plane "Equatorial plane") and the [normal](/wiki/Normal_(geometry) "Normal (geometry)") from the ground at that location.

Longitude is generally given using the [geodetic normal](/wiki/Geodetic_normal "Geodetic normal") or the [gravity direction](/wiki/Gravity_direction "Gravity direction"). The **astronomical longitude** can differ slightly from the ordinary longitude because of *[vertical deflection](/wiki/Vertical_deflection "Vertical deflection")*, small variations in Earth's gravitational field (see [astronomical latitude](/wiki/Astronomical_latitude "Astronomical latitude")).

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Longitude&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of longitude](/wiki/History_of_longitude "History of longitude")

The concept of longitude was first developed by ancient Greek astronomers. [Hipparchus](/wiki/Hipparchus "Hipparchus") (2nd century BC) used a coordinate system that assumed a spherical Earth, and divided it into 360° as is still done today. His [prime meridian](/wiki/Prime_meridian "Prime meridian") passed through [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria").[[3]](#cite_note-Dicks-3): 31 He also proposed a method of determining longitude by comparing the local time of a [lunar eclipse](/wiki/Lunar_eclipse "Lunar eclipse") at two different places, thus demonstrating an understanding of the relationship between longitude and time.[[3]](#cite_note-Dicks-3): 11[[4]](#cite_note-4) [Claudius Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy") (2nd century AD) developed a mapping system using curved parallels that reduced distortion. He also collected data for many locations, from Britain to the Middle East. He used a prime meridian through the Canary Islands, so that all longitude values would be positive. While Ptolemy's system was sound, the data he used were often poor, leading to a gross over-estimate (by about 70%) of the length of the Mediterranean.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Bunbury-6): 551–553[[7]](#cite_note-7)

After the fall of the Roman Empire, interest in geography greatly declined in Europe.[[8]](#cite_note-Wright1925-8): 65 Hindu and Muslim astronomers continued to develop these ideas, adding many new locations and often improving on Ptolemy's data.[[9]](#cite_note-Ragep-9)[[10]](#cite_note-Tibbett-10) For example, [al-Battānī](/wiki/Al-Battani "Al-Battani") used simultaneous observations of two lunar eclipses to determine the difference in longitude between [Antakya](/wiki/Antakya "Antakya") and [Raqqa](/wiki/Raqqa "Raqqa") with an error of less than 1°. This is considered to be the best that can be achieved with the methods then available: observation of the eclipse with the naked eye, and determination of local time using an [astrolabe](/wiki/Astrolabe "Astrolabe") to measure the altitude of a suitable "clock star".[[11]](#cite_note-Said2-11)[[12]](#cite_note-Steele-12)

In the later Middle Ages, interest in geography revived in the west, as travel increased, and Arab scholarship began to be known through contact with Spain and North Africa. In the 12th century, astronomical tables were prepared for a number of European cities, based on the work of [al-Zarqālī](/wiki/Ab%C5%AB_Ish%C4%81q_Ibr%C4%81h%C4%ABm_al-Zarq%C4%81l%C4%AB "Abū Ishāq Ibrāhīm al-Zarqālī") in [Toledo](/wiki/Toledo,_Spain "Toledo, Spain"). The lunar eclipse of September 12, 1178 was used to establish the longitude differences between Toledo, [Marseilles](/wiki/Marseille "Marseille"), and [Hereford](/wiki/Hereford "Hereford").[[13]](#cite_note-Wright1923-13): 85

[Christopher Columbus](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus "Christopher Columbus") made two attempts to use lunar eclipses to discover his longitude, the first in [Saona Island](/wiki/Saona_Island "Saona Island"), on 14 September 1494 (second voyage), and the second in [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica") on 29 February 1504 (fourth voyage). It is assumed that he used astronomical tables for reference. His determinations of longitude showed large errors of 13° and 38° W respectively.[[14]](#cite_note-Pickering-14) Randles (1985) documents longitude measurement by the Portuguese and Spanish between 1514 and 1627 both in the Americas and Asia. Errors ranged from 2° to 25°.[[15]](#cite_note-Randles-15)

The telescope was invented in the early 17th century. Initially an observation device, developments over the next half century transformed it into an accurate measurement tool.[[16]](#cite_note-Pannekoek-16)[[17]](#cite_note-Van_Helden-17) The [pendulum clock](/wiki/Pendulum_clock "Pendulum clock") was patented by [Christiaan Huygens](/wiki/Christiaan_Huygens "Christiaan Huygens") in 1657[[18]](#cite_note-Grimbergen-18) and gave an increase in accuracy of about 30 fold over previous mechanical clocks.[[19]](#cite_note-19) These two inventions would revolutionise observational astronomy and cartography.[[20]](#cite_note-Olmsted-20)

On land, the period from the development of telescopes and pendulum clocks until the mid-18th century saw a steady increase in the number of places whose longitude had been determined with reasonable accuracy, often with errors of less than a degree, and nearly always within 2° to 3°. By the 1720s errors were consistently less than 1°.[[21]](#cite_note-21) At sea during the same period, the situation was very different. Two problems proved intractable; the first was the need of a navigator for immediate results, and the second was the marine environment. Making accurate observations in an ocean swell is much harder than on land, and pendulum clocks do not work well in these conditions.

### The Chronometer

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Longitude&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: The Chronometer")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Harrison_H4_clockwork_1.jpg/330px-Harrison_H4_clockwork_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Harrison_H4_clockwork_1.jpg)

The clockwork in [John Harrison](/wiki/John_Harrison "John Harrison")'s [H4 marine chronometer](/wiki/Marine_chronometer#First_examples "Marine chronometer") on display at the [Royal Observatory, Greenwich](/wiki/Royal_Observatory,_Greenwich "Royal Observatory, Greenwich")

In response to the problems of navigation, a number of European maritime powers offered prizes for a method to determine longitude at sea. The best-known of these is the [Longitude Act](/wiki/Longitude_Act "Longitude Act") passed by the British parliament in 1714.[[22]](#cite_note-Siegel-22): 8 It offered two levels of rewards, for solutions within 1° and 0.5°. Rewards were given for two solutions: lunar distances, made practicable by the tables of [Tobias Mayer](/wiki/Tobias_Mayer "Tobias Mayer")[[23]](#cite_note-Forbes2006-23) developed into a [nautical almanac](/wiki/Nautical_almanac "Nautical almanac") by the [Astronomer Royal](/wiki/Astronomer_Royal "Astronomer Royal") [Nevil Maskelyne](/wiki/Nevil_Maskelyne "Nevil Maskelyne"); and for the [chronometers](/wiki/Chronometers "Chronometers") developed by the Yorkshire carpenter and clock-maker [John Harrison](/wiki/John_Harrison "John Harrison"). Harrison built five chronometers over more than three decades. This work was supported and rewarded with thousands of pounds from the Board of Longitude,[[24]](#cite_note-24) but he fought to receive money up to the top reward of £20,000, finally receiving an additional payment in 1773 after the intervention of Parliament.[[22]](#cite_note-Siegel-22): 26 It was some while before either method became widely used in navigation. In the early years, chronometers were very expensive, and the calculations required for lunar distances were still complex and time-consuming. Lunar distances came into general use after 1790.[[25]](#cite_note-Wess2015-25) Chronometers had the advantages that both the observations and the calculations were simpler, and as they became cheaper in the early 19th century they started to replace lunars, which were seldom used after 1850.[[26]](#cite_note-Littlehales-26)

The first working [telegraphs](/wiki/Telegraphy "Telegraphy") were established in Britain by [Wheatstone](/wiki/Charles_Wheatstone "Charles Wheatstone") and [Cooke](/wiki/William_Fothergill_Cooke "William Fothergill Cooke") in 1839, and in the US by [Morse](/wiki/Samuel_Morse "Samuel Morse") in 1844. It was quickly realised that the telegraph could be used to transmit a time signal for longitude determination.[[27]](#cite_note-Walker_1850-27) The method was soon in practical use for longitude determination, especially in North America, and over longer and longer distances as the telegraph network expanded, including western Europe with the completion of transatlantic cables. The United States Coast Survey, renamed the [United States Coast and Geodetic Survey](/wiki/United_States_Coast_and_Geodetic_Survey "United States Coast and Geodetic Survey") in 1878, was particularly active in this development, and not just in the United States. The Survey established chains of mapped locations through Central and South America, and the West Indies, and as far as Japan and China in the years 1874–90. This contributed greatly to the accurate mapping of these areas.[[28]](#cite_note-Knox-28)[[29]](#cite_note-Green1883-29)

While mariners benefited from the accurate charts, they could not receive telegraph signals while under way, and so could not use the method for navigation. This changed when wireless telegraphy (radio) became available in the early 20th century.[[30]](#cite_note-Munro1902-30) Wireless time signals for the use of ships were transmitted from [Halifax, Nova Scotia](/wiki/Halifax,_Nova_Scotia "Halifax, Nova Scotia"), starting in 1907[[31]](#cite_note-Hutchnson-31) and from the [Eiffel Tower](/wiki/Eiffel_Tower "Eiffel Tower") in Paris from 1910.[[32]](#cite_note-Lockyer1913-32) These signals allowed navigators to check and adjust their chronometers frequently.[[33]](#cite_note-Zimmerman-33)

[Radio navigation](/wiki/Radio_navigation "Radio navigation") systems came into general use after [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). The systems all depended on transmissions from fixed navigational beacons. A ship-board receiver calculated the vessel's position from these transmissions.[[34]](#cite_note-Pierce-34) They allowed accurate navigation when poor visibility prevented astronomical observations, and became the established method for commercial shipping until replaced by [GPS](/wiki/Global_Positioning_System "Global Positioning System") in the early 1990s.

## Determination

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Longitude&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Determination")]

Further information: [Celestial navigation § Longitude](/wiki/Celestial_navigation#Longitude "Celestial navigation"), and [History\_of\_longitude § Methods\_of\_determining\_longitude](/wiki/History_of_longitude#Methods_of_determining_longitude "History of longitude")

See also: [Latitude determination](/wiki/Latitude_determination "Latitude determination")

The main conventional methods for determining longitude are listed below. With one exception (magnetic declination), they all depend on a common principle, which is to determine the time for an event or measurement and to compare it with the time at a different location. Longitude, being up to 180° east or west of a [prime meridian](/wiki/Prime_meridian "Prime meridian"), is mathematically related to time differences up to 12 hours by a factor of 15. Thus, a time differential (in hours) between two points is multiplied by 15 to obtain a longitudinal difference (in degrees).

Historically, times used for calculating longitude have included [apparent solar time](/wiki/Apparent_solar_time "Apparent solar time"), [local mean time](/wiki/Local_mean_time "Local mean time"), and [ephemeris time](/wiki/Ephemeris_time "Ephemeris time"), with mean time being the one most used for navigation of the sea. See also the [equation of time](/wiki/Equation_of_time "Equation of time") for details on the differences.

* [Lunar distances](/wiki/Lunar_distance_(navigation) "Lunar distance (navigation)") and Moon [culminations](/wiki/Culmination "Culmination"). In its orbit around the Earth, the Moon moves relative to the stars at a rate of just over 0.5°/hour. The angle between the limb of the Moon and a suitable star, planet, or sun is measured with a [sextant](/wiki/Sextant "Sextant"), and, after consulting [ephemeris](/wiki/Ephemeris "Ephemeris") tables, a value for the [mean time](/wiki/Local_mean_time "Local mean time") at a reference meridian, usually Greenwich, can be calculated. For a culmination, the observer simply records the time and compares it with the reference time in the ephemerides after correcting for [refraction](/wiki/Refraction "Refraction") and other errors. This method was established by [Nathaniel Pigott](/wiki/Nathaniel_Pigott "Nathaniel Pigott") around 1786.[[35]](#cite_note-time-35) *[The Nautical Almanac](/wiki/The_Nautical_Almanac "The Nautical Almanac")* was published in the UK beginning in 1767 and the *[American Ephemeris and Nautical Almanac](/wiki/American_Ephemeris_and_Nautical_Almanac "American Ephemeris and Nautical Almanac")* starting in 1852.
* Satellites of Jupiter. [Galileo](/wiki/Galileo_Galilei "Galileo Galilei") proposed that with sufficiently accurate knowledge of the orbits of the satellites, their positions could provide a measure of absolute time. The method requires a telescope, as the moons are not visible to the naked eye. Ephemeris tables are employed for comparison to a reference meridian.
* Appulses, occultations, transits, and eclipses. An [appulse](/wiki/Appulse "Appulse") is the least apparent distance between two objects (the Moon, a star or a planet); an [occultation](/wiki/Occultation "Occultation") occurs when a star or planet passes behind the Moon — essentially a type of eclipse. Lunar eclipses continued to be used. The times of these events are compared to those of a reference meridian. Major observatories used the [transit circle or meridian circle](/wiki/Meridian_circle "Meridian circle") to establish very accurate longitude values for their country, often establishing their own [prime meridian](/wiki/Prime_meridian "Prime meridian") at the longitude of the instrument.[[36]](#cite_note-transit-36)
* Transport of [chronometers](/wiki/Marine_chronometer "Marine chronometer"). A clock is set to the local time of a starting point whose longitude is known, and the longitude of any other place can be determined by comparing its [local mean time](/wiki/Local_mean_time "Local mean time") with the clock time. While marine chronometers are relatively stable, they are also relatively large and expensive. Prior to the quartz crystal, chronometers were susceptible to time drift from temperature fluctuations and vibration.
* Signals. Rockets and lights were occasionally used in the 18th and 19th century, although the method is impractical except for short distances and demonstrations.[[35]](#cite_note-time-35) It was a rudimentary form of [synchronizing time](/wiki/Time_and_frequency_transfer "Time and frequency transfer") and establishing longitude. However, [signaling by "ball drop"](/wiki/Time_ball "Time ball") was extensively used in the US Navy and Royal Navy in the 19th century. In each case, there were observatories near bodies of water that would drop a ball from a tower, alerting the ships of the correct time, and hence enabling them to maintain stable longitudinal position fixes while at sea.[[37]](#cite_note-ball-37)
* [Telegraphic](/wiki/Telegraph "Telegraph") determination of longitude. First suggested by the American astronomer [Sears Cook Walker](/wiki/Sears_Cook_Walker "Sears Cook Walker"), the [United States Coast Survey](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Survey "United States Coast Survey") began deploying it in 1849.[[35]](#cite_note-time-35) Europe quickly followed. As the American West was settled, mapping and surveying was greatly improved by the use of the telegraph to determine time and longitude differences between stations. The laying of [transatlantic telegraph cables](/wiki/Transatlantic_telegraph_cable "Transatlantic telegraph cable") also helped establish coordinated global mapping and navigation.
* [Magnetic declination](/wiki/Magnetic_declination "Magnetic declination"). A compass needle does not in general point [true north](/wiki/True_north "True north"). The variation from true north varies with location, and it was suggested that this could provide a basis for determination of longitude.

With the exception of magnetic declination, all proved practicable methods. Developments on land and sea, however, were very different.

Several newer methods for navigation, location finding, and the determination of longitude exist. [Radio navigation](/wiki/Radio_navigation "Radio navigation"), [satellite navigation](/wiki/Satellite_navigation "Satellite navigation"), and [Inertial navigation systems](/wiki/Inertial_navigation_system "Inertial navigation system"), along with [celestial navigation](/wiki/Celestial_navigation "Celestial navigation"), are a few of the more prevalent ones.

## Values

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Longitude&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Values")]

Longitude is given as an [angular measurement](/wiki/Angle "Angle") with 0° at the [Prime Meridian](/wiki/Prime_Meridian "Prime Meridian"), ranging from −180° westward to +180° eastward. The Greek letter λ (lambda)[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39) is used to denote the location of a place on Earth east or west of the Prime Meridian.

Each degree of longitude is sub-divided into 60 [minutes](/wiki/Minute_of_arc "Minute of arc"), each of which is divided into 60 [seconds](/wiki/Arcsecond "Arcsecond"). A longitude is thus specified in [sexagesimal](/wiki/Sexagesimal "Sexagesimal") notation as, for example, 23° 27′ 30″ E. For higher precision, the seconds are specified with a [decimal fraction](/wiki/Decimal_fraction "Decimal fraction"). An alternative representation uses degrees and minutes, and parts of a minute are expressed in decimal notation, thus: 23° 27.5′ E. Degrees may also be expressed as a decimal fraction: 23.45833° E. For calculations, the angular measure may be converted to [radians](/wiki/Radian "Radian"), so longitude may also be expressed in this manner as a signed fraction of π ([pi](/wiki/Pi "Pi")), or an unsigned fraction of 2π.

For calculations, the west/east suffix is replaced by a negative sign in the [Western Hemisphere](/wiki/Western_Hemisphere "Western Hemisphere"). The international standard convention ([ISO 6709](/wiki/ISO_6709 "ISO 6709"))—that east is positive—is consistent with a right-handed [Cartesian coordinate system](/wiki/Cartesian_coordinate_system "Cartesian coordinate system"), with the North Pole up. A specific longitude may then be combined with a specific latitude (positive in the [Northern Hemisphere](/wiki/Northern_Hemisphere "Northern Hemisphere")) to give a precise position on the Earth's surface. Confusingly, the convention of negative for east is also sometimes seen, most commonly in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"); the [Earth System Research Laboratories](/wiki/Earth_System_Research_Laboratories "Earth System Research Laboratories") used it on an older version of one of their pages, in order "to make coordinate entry less awkward" for applications confined to the [Western Hemisphere](/wiki/Western_Hemisphere "Western Hemisphere"). They have since shifted to the standard approach.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

The longitude is [singular](/wiki/Mathematical_singularity "Mathematical singularity") at the [Poles](/wiki/Geographical_pole "Geographical pole") and calculations that are sufficiently accurate for other positions may be inaccurate at or near the Poles. Also the [discontinuity](/wiki/Discontinuity_(mathematics) "Discontinuity (mathematics)") at the ±[180° meridian](/wiki/180th_meridian "180th meridian") must be handled with care in calculations. An example is a calculation of east displacement by subtracting two longitudes, which gives the wrong answer if the two positions are on either side of this meridian. To avoid these complexities, some applications use another [horizontal position representation](/wiki/Horizontal_position_representation "Horizontal position representation").

## Length of a degree of longitude

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Longitude&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Length of a degree of longitude")]

See also: [Length of a degree of latitude](/wiki/Length_of_a_degree_of_latitude "Length of a degree of latitude")

The length of a degree of longitude (east–west distance) depends only on the radius of a circle of latitude. For a sphere of radius a that radius at latitude φ is *a* [cos](/wiki/Cosine "Cosine") *φ*, and the length of a one-degree (or ⁠π/180⁠ [radian](/wiki/Radian "Radian")) arc along a circle of latitude is
![{\displaystyle \Delta _{\rm {long}}^{1}={\frac {\pi }{180}}a\cos \phi }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e88fc2a6f8daa8f14d5a2b4cbbaa19e69a12ea86)

| φ | Δ1 lat | Δ1 long |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 0° | 110.574 km | 111.320 km |
| 15° | 110.649 km | 107.551 km |
| 30° | 110.852 km | 96.486 km |
| 45° | 111.133 km | 78.847 km |
| 60° | 111.412 km | 55.800 km |
| 75° | 111.618 km | 28.902 km |
| 90° | 111.694 km | 0.000 km |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/WGS84_angle_to_distance_conversion.svg/330px-WGS84_angle_to_distance_conversion.svg.png)](/wiki/File:WGS84_angle_to_distance_conversion.svg)

Length of one degree (black), minute (blue) and second (red) of latitude and longitude in metric (upper half) and imperial units (lower half) at a given latitude (vertical axis) in WGS84. For example, the green arrows show that Donetsk (green circle) at 48°N has a Δlong of 74.63 km/° (1.244 km/min, 20.73 m/sec etc) and a Δlat of 111.2 km/° (1.853 km/min, 30.89 m/sec etc).

When the Earth is modelled by an [ellipsoid](/wiki/Ellipsoid "Ellipsoid") this arc length becomes[[41]](#cite_note-osborne-41)[[42]](#cite_note-rapp-42)
![{\displaystyle \Delta _{\rm {long}}^{1}={\frac {\pi a\cos \phi }{180{\sqrt {1-e^{2}\sin ^{2}\phi }}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/00f543b7279e3855f09adc960eb18aebb46a9768)
where e, the eccentricity of the ellipsoid, is related to the major and minor axes (the equatorial and polar radii respectively) by
![{\displaystyle e^{2}={\frac {a^{2}-b^{2}}{a^{2}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/acc915e4db2a15442229073d080670744d0f1c94)

An alternative formula is
![{\displaystyle \Delta _{\rm {long}}^{1}={\frac {\pi }{180}}a\cos \beta \quad {\mbox{where }}\tan \beta ={\frac {b}{a}}\tan \phi }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/915b3d9fc2b11186d49b7641aa557cc1b3ab2ced)

Here ![{\displaystyle \beta }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7ed48a5e36207156fb792fa79d29925d2f7901e8) is the so-called [*parametric* or *reduced* latitude](/wiki/Parametric_latitude "Parametric latitude").

cos φ decreases from 1 at the equator to 0 at the poles, which measures how circles of latitude shrink from the equator to a point at the pole, so the length of a degree of longitude decreases likewise. This contrasts with the small (1%) increase in the [length of a degree of latitude](/wiki/Length_of_a_degree_of_latitude "Length of a degree of latitude") (north–south distance), equator to pole. The table shows both for the [WGS84](/wiki/WGS84 "WGS84") ellipsoid with a = 6378137.0 m and b = 6356752.3142 m. The distance between two points 1 degree apart on the same circle of latitude, measured along that circle of latitude, is slightly more than the shortest ([geodesic](/wiki/Geodesic "Geodesic")) distance between those points (unless on the equator, where these are equal); the difference is less than 0.6 m (2 ft).

A [geographical mile](/wiki/Geographical_mile "Geographical mile") is defined as the length of one [minute of arc](/wiki/Minute_of_arc "Minute of arc") along the equator (one equatorial minute of longitude) therefore a degree of longitude along the equator is exactly 60 geographical miles or 111.3 kilometers, as there are 60 minutes in a degree. The length of 1 minute of longitude along the equator is 1 geographical mile or 1.855 km or 1.153 miles, while the length of 1 second of it is 0.016 geographical mile or 30.916 m or 101.43 feet.
